title: Reintegrating Child Soldiers
region: Global / Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, Middle East
starter_prompts: How do I safely approach a child who has just separated from an armed group?
What are the biggest barriers to community acceptance of returning child soldiers?
How should I handle a situation where a child is both a victim and has committed serious violence?
What are the specific needs of girls associated with armed groups that standard DDR programs miss?
How do I assess whether a child is genuinely demobilized or still under the influence of a commander?

situation_overview: Children associated with armed forces and groups (CAAFAG) — often called child soldiers — are among the most complex beneficiary populations in humanitarian and stabilization programming. The Paris Principles (2007) define a child associated with an armed force or group as any person below 18 who is or who has been recruited or used by an armed force or armed group in any capacity, including but not limited to children used as fighters, cooks, porters, messengers, spies, or for sexual purposes. This definition is deliberately broad: the majority of children associated with armed groups are not combatants, and most reintegration failures stem from programming that focuses narrowly on former fighters and ignores children in support roles. Globally, the UN Secretary-General's annual reports on children and armed conflict document six grave violations: killing and maiming, recruitment and use, sexual violence, abduction, attacks on schools and hospitals, and denial of humanitarian access. Reintegration programming sits at the intersection of all six. The Betancourt longitudinal study in Sierra Leone — the most rigorous research on outcomes for former child soldiers — found that social acceptance and community support are better predictors of long-term psychosocial health than the severity of what children experienced or perpetrated during armed group membership. A 2025 working paper by OSRSG-CAAC and Princeton University's LISD advances the field further by establishing child-inclusive, gender-responsive, and intersectional frameworks as operational requirements — recognizing that a child's gender, age, disability, ethnicity, and socioeconomic status interact to shape vulnerability and must be addressed together. The UN SG's 2024 report on the Central African Republic documents over 14,000 children verified as released from armed groups between 2014-2023, yet recruitment continued simultaneously by the same groups — illustrating that in fragmented conflict environments, reintegration is rarely a linear process. Field operators working in this space must navigate significant tensions: between child protection principles and justice/accountability frameworks; between the needs of former CAAFAG and the communities that may have suffered at their hands; between the urgency of extracting children from armed groups and the dangers of premature family reunification; and between Western-imported psychosocial models and culturally embedded healing practices. Standard adult DDR frameworks consistently fail children — children require specialized programming that addresses trauma, education deficits, family dynamics, and community attitudes simultaneously.

armed_groups.name: State Armed Forces with Child Soldiers
armed_groups.geography: Documented cases include Myanmar (Tatmadaw), South Sudan (SSPDF), Somalia (SNA and clan militias), DRC (FARDC), Afghanistan (ANA/ANP, pre-2021). State actors are subject to UN monitoring under Security Council Resolution 1612 and subsequent resolutions.
armed_groups.status: Under the 1612 monitoring and reporting mechanism, state armed forces that recruit or use children can be listed in the UN Secretary-General's annual report, triggering action plans and engagement with the UN Country Task Force on Monitoring and Reporting (CTFMR). Action Plans are formal commitments by listed parties to end and prevent the six grave violations. As of 2024, several national armies have signed Action Plans; compliance and verification capacity varies significantly by country.
armed_groups.engagement_notes: Engagement with state armed forces on child soldier release requires careful coordination with the UN CTFMR and the OSRSG-CAAC. Bilateral pressure from donor governments can be effective. Do not conduct independent demobilization negotiations with state forces without CTFMR coordination — it can undermine ongoing processes and create legal liability.
armed_groups.risks: Children who escape or are released from state armed forces may be re-recruited, detained as security threats, or face retaliation from commanders. Governments may deny child recruitment publicly while it continues in practice. Verify releases through child protection actors on the ground, not through official government channels.

armed_groups.name: Non-State Armed Groups (NSAGs) with Child Soldiers
armed_groups.type: Armed non-state actors / varied engagement profiles
armed_groups.geography: Documented across DRC (multiple groups including M23, FDLR, ADF), Central African Republic (multiple groups), South Sudan (SPLA-IO and other factions), Somalia (al-Shabaab, clan militias), Myanmar (multiple ethnic armed organizations and PDFs), Nigeria (Boko Haram / ISWAP, Lake Chad Basin), Mali/Burkina Faso/Niger (JNIM, GSIM), Yemen (Houthis, Southern Transitional Council-aligned forces), Colombia (FARC dissidents, ELN).
armed_groups.status: NSAGs are listed in the SG's annual report on CAAFAG and are subject to engagement through Geneva Call's Deed of Commitment on children. As of 2024, over 50 NSAGs have signed Geneva Call commitments. Al-Shabaab and ISWAP affiliates are among the most active child recruiters; they do not engage with Geneva Call or UN monitoring mechanisms. FARC dissidents in Colombia have resumed recruitment after the peace process partial collapse.
armed_groups.engagement_notes: Engagement with NSAGs for child releases requires humanitarian intermediaries (UNICEF, ICRC) and, where possible, Geneva Call. Direct negotiations by implementing NGOs without established trust and humanitarian identity can endanger both staff and children. Understand the NSAG's decision-making structure — some have central commanders who can order releases; others are fragmented and releases must be negotiated with local commanders individually.
armed_groups.risks: ⚠️ NSAGs may use child release negotiations as cover for intelligence collection on protection actor networks, child locations, or resettlement sites. Children released by NSAGs may still be under surveillance or loyalty pressure from commanders. Some NSAGs 'release' children who remain effectively under coercive relationships with individual commanders. Verify genuine separation before initiating reintegration programming.

armed_groups.name: Girls Associated with Armed Forces and Groups (GAAFG)
armed_groups.type: Special population within CAAFAG / frequently invisible in standard programming
armed_groups.geography: Present in virtually all documented CAAFAG contexts. Frequently undercounted: in Sierra Leone, girls represented an estimated 30% of CAAFAG but less than 5% of DDR program participants. In Colombia, girls represented up to 40% of FARC's child membership but were systematically undercounted in formal demobilization processes.
armed_groups.status: Girls associated with armed groups are consistently the most underserved population in DDR programming for three reasons: (1) they often do not self-identify as 'soldiers' even if they performed combat functions; (2) formal DDR verification systems are biased toward males who can present weapons; (3) they face specific stigma (sexual abuse, 'bush wives,' children born in captivity) that makes them reluctant to enter formal DDR processes. The Paris Principles explicitly call for gender-sensitive programming, but implementation remains weak.
armed_groups.engagement_notes: Separate intake and assessment processes for girls. Female caseworkers at all stages. Recognize that girls may present as dependents of male commanders and may not self-identify as CAAFAG. Assess for forced marriage, sexual violence, and children born in captivity as routine screening items, not exceptional flags.
armed_groups.risks: ⚠️ Girls who were sexually exploited by commanders face double stigma: community rejection as 'dirty' or 'used,' and continued coercive relationships with commanders who may claim paternity of their children. Rush to family reunification without preparation of the family can result in girls being rejected, re-victimized, or returned to armed groups as the only community willing to accept them. Children born in captivity face identity and belonging crises that persist across generations.

armed_groups.name: Children Recruited by Terrorist-Designated Groups
armed_groups.type: CAAFAG in counter-terrorism contexts / highest legal complexity
armed_groups.geography: Al-Shabaab (Somalia), ISWAP/Boko Haram (Lake Chad Basin), ISIS affiliates (Syria, Iraq, Sahel), Taliban (Afghanistan, pre-2021).
armed_groups.status: Children recruited by groups designated as terrorist organizations face the most severe reintegration challenges: host governments may treat them as security threats; donor governments with counter-terrorism legislation may restrict funding for their reintegration; communities may refuse them return; and formal DDR systems often exclude them in favor of criminal prosecution frameworks. In Somalia, children captured from al-Shabaab are frequently held in adult detention facilities. UN Security Council Resolution 2427 (2018) explicitly reaffirms that reintegration of children recruited by terrorist-designated groups should be primarily treated as a child protection matter.
armed_groups.engagement_notes: Child protection legal frameworks are the primary operative framework — UNCRC, Paris Principles, and the CRC Optional Protocol on children in armed conflict apply regardless of the group's terrorist designation. Advocate for separation of children from adult detention; push for national juvenile justice frameworks that treat children's recruitment as an aggravating factor against recruiters, not as a criminal act by the child.
armed_groups.risks: ⚠️ Counter-terrorism financing restrictions in the United States (18 USC 2339B) and equivalent EU legislation can be interpreted to restrict funding for reintegration programming that benefits former members of designated groups, even children. Before programming, verify that your donor agreements and national law permit activity in this space. OFAC and EU sanctions carve-outs for humanitarian work exist but are narrow and require legal review.

lessons: The Paris Principles (2007) are the foundational normative framework for all CAAFAG programming — read them before designing any intervention. Key operational provisions: (1) the definition of 'child associated with armed forces or groups' is broad — it includes all children in support roles, not just fighters; (2) children should never be treated primarily as perpetrators; (3) all actions must be guided by the best interests of the child; (4) family unity should be the goal unless it creates risk; (5) girls require specific and dedicated programming. The Paris Principles supersede the earlier Cape Town Principles (1997) and are the reference document for CTFMR and UNICEF engagement.

The UN monitoring and reporting mechanism (MRM) established by SCRES 1612 (2005) is the primary international accountability mechanism for grave violations against children in armed conflict. It operates through Country Task Forces on Monitoring and Reporting (CTFMRs) co-chaired by UNICEF and the DSRSG in each country. Understand how the MRM operates in your country context before engaging — it determines who is listed in the SG's annual report and who has signed Action Plans. Engagement with listed parties without CTFMR coordination can interfere with ongoing processes.

Age determination is a pervasive operational challenge: birth documentation is frequently unavailable in CAAFAG contexts, and armed groups may have altered children's documentation to obscure recruitment of minors. The international standard is that in cases of age doubt, the individual should be presumed to be a child and treated as such until proven otherwise. Do not impose minimum age cutoffs for DDR eligibility that exclude children whose ages cannot be verified. Use a combination of physical assessment, social history, and contextual information — not a single method.

Children cannot legally consent to their own recruitment under international law. This matters operationally: when a child tells you they 'joined voluntarily,' this is legally and psychologically complex. Research on voluntariness (Denov, Wessells) consistently finds that apparent 'voluntary' recruitment is almost always a response to coercion, incentive, or the absence of viable alternatives. Do not use a child's stated voluntariness to reduce their eligibility for reintegration programming or to reduce the gravity of accountability for recruiters.

UN Security Council Resolution 2427 (2018) advances the normative framework in three key areas: (1) it calls for children's release and reintegration to be prioritized even before formal peace processes; (2) it calls on states to treat children primarily through juvenile justice frameworks, not adult criminal prosecution; (3) it affirms that children recruited by terrorist-designated groups should be treated primarily as victims. Cite 2427 when advocating against detention of children or restriction of programming due to counter-terrorism concerns.

The 2025 Princeton LISD / OSRSG-CAAC working paper on child-inclusive and gender-responsive reintegration is the most current synthesis of operational guidance and normative standards. It advances the field in three areas: (1) it applies an intersectional framework — recognizing that a child's gender, age, disability status, ethnicity, religion, and socioeconomic background interact to shape reintegration outcomes, and that single-axis programming consistently misses the most vulnerable; (2) it provides concrete programmatic specifications for gender-responsive programming at each stage from release through durable integration; (3) it identifies persistent data gaps — particularly in gender-disaggregated outcome data — that prevent evidence-based program design. Use it as a program design checklist alongside the Paris Principles when entering any new CAAFAG context.

lessons.category: Release and Separation from Armed Groups
lessons: Release is not the same as reintegration — and confusing the two is the most common operational error. Release is the moment a child separates from an armed group. Reintegration is a process that takes months to years. A child who has been released but not reintegrated is more likely to be re-recruited than one who never left, because they have been marked by their association without gaining the support systems that make civilian life viable. Resource allocation must reflect this: release events get attention, but sustained reintegration programming is where outcomes are determined.

Never attempt to extract a child from an armed group without prior coordination with UNICEF, the ICRC, and the CTFMR. Unauthorized extractions endanger the child, the extracting organization, and other children in the group (who may face reprisals). The sequencing is: (1) UNICEF or ICRC negotiates access and release conditions with the armed group; (2) child protection actors establish interim care arrangements before release; (3) release occurs at a pre-agreed location with child protection actors present; (4) interim care begins immediately upon release. Do not compress or skip steps under time pressure.

Children frequently do not self-identify as CAAFAG at the point of separation. They may present as ordinary civilians, displaced persons, or dependents of adults. Screening for CAAFAG status should be part of routine intake for all children in displacement, detention, or emergency settings — not only at formal DDR cantonment sites. Signs include: inability to account for time during the conflict period, distrust of civilian authority, deference to hierarchical commands, physical markers (calluses, posture, injuries), and reluctance to discuss the conflict period.

Cantonment-based DDR is poorly suited to children. Classic adult DDR (cantonment → weapons handover → registration → demobilization) fails children because: (1) many children enter armed groups without weapons; (2) children may be reluctant to congregate in military-style facilities; (3) girls are systematically excluded. Community-based DDR — where children are screened and supported within communities, without cantonment — produces better reintegration outcomes and reaches girls at much higher rates (IAWG-DDR IDDRS 5.30). Push back against government or donor insistence on cantonment-only approaches for children.

The presence of adult ex-combatants during children's release and transit creates protection risks. Children who have been under the command authority of specific adults may continue to follow orders from those adults even after formal separation. Separate children from adult ex-combatants during transit, interim care, and initial reintegration. Brief children explicitly: they are now civilians, the armed group structure no longer has authority over them, and they can refuse commands from former commanders.

Interim care facilities (ICFs) or transit centers provide the bridge between release and family reintegration. Minimum standards: sex-separated sleeping areas, child-only spaces (no adults with unclear status), female child protection staff present at all times (especially overnight), medical screening and treatment, adequate nutrition, psychosocial support activities, and beginning of family tracing. ICFs should last weeks to months — not years. Children who remain in institutional care for extended periods develop dependency and institutional behaviors that impede community reintegration.

The initial assessment period is the most critical investment for long-term reintegration outcomes. Assess: (1) physical health and medical needs including sexual and reproductive health, without stigma; (2) mental health using validated, culturally adapted tools — not standard Western PTSD checklists, which may not capture local symptom expressions; (3) social history: family composition, relationships before armed group membership, history within the group; (4) education level and learning gaps; (5) livelihood skills; (6) protection risks in the proposed return community. Assessment must be done with trained, consistent caseworkers — not in intake-style group interviews.

Children who have committed serious violence require specialized, non-judgmental engagement. Research (Betancourt, Drumbl, Wessells) consistently finds that: (1) children who perpetrated violence during armed group membership do not differ significantly in psychosocial outcomes from children who did not, if they receive strong social support; (2) community awareness of what a child did significantly affects acceptance outcomes; (3) children who are required to deny or hide their histories have worse long-term outcomes than those who can process and contextualize them. Do not design reintegration programs built on concealment of children's histories.

Trauma-informed practice in CAAFAG contexts requires attention to the difference between event-based trauma (PTSD) and complex developmental trauma from prolonged exposure and identity formation within an armed group. Many former CAAFAG have spent formative developmental years within armed structures — their sense of identity, belonging, competence, and relational patterns were formed there. Standard trauma-focused CBT designed for single-event trauma is insufficient. Long-term, relationship-based therapeutic support combined with meaningful roles in civilian society is more effective than clinical trauma processing alone.

⚠️ Children with children: girls who have children born in captivity arrive with protection needs in two generations simultaneously. The child's child is also a victim of the armed group context and requires their own assessment and support. Do not separate mothers from infants as a condition of program entry. Ensure food rations and medical care include infants. Assess the child-mother's capacity to parent while also receiving her own support — these roles may be in tension for adolescent girls.

Research on former child soldiers' own perceptions of reintegration (Brownell, 2020, Liberia) reveals three themes that practitioners consistently underestimate: dissatisfaction (unmet economic and social needs that formal programs promised but did not deliver), powerlessness (a pervasive sense of lacking agency over their own reintegration trajectory), and ambivalence about reintegration itself (some children did not experience civilian life as clearly preferable to armed group life, due to economic marginality and social rejection). These are not program design failures to rationalize — they are signals that programs are not closing the gap between promise and reality. Incorporate former CAAFAG as active co-designers of reintegration programming from the assessment stage forward, not passive beneficiaries whose experiences are recorded but not acted upon.

Family reunification is the goal of reintegration, but premature reunification is one of the most common reintegration failures. Reunification should be preceded by: (1) family tracing to locate family members; (2) family assessment to determine whether the return environment is safe; (3) family preparation — parents and siblings should receive information about what the child experienced and preparation for how they may present; (4) the child's informed consent; and (5) post-reunification follow-up visits. Skipping any of these steps to expedite family contact increases the risk of re-separation, rejection, and re-recruitment.

Family rejection is a significant risk, particularly for girls, for children who perpetrated violence in their home community, and for children who have been away for extended periods. Family rejection rates vary by context — Sierra Leone studies found rejection rates of 20-30% for girls. Rejection is not permanent: in many cases, community mediation, economic support to families, and time reduce initial rejection. Do not classify rejection as a case closed — continue follow-up and mediation. Prepare children in advance for the possibility that reunification may not happen immediately.

Mediation rituals and traditional reconciliation mechanisms are frequently more effective than formal counseling for facilitating family and community reintegration. In Mozambique, the 'cleansing' rituals performed by traditional healers were critical to community acceptance of former child soldiers — not Western-trained counselors (Honwana, Boothby). In Uganda, the Acholi 'stepping on the egg' ceremony facilitated community reintegration of LRA abductees. Invest in identifying, understanding, and working with existing community-level healing mechanisms. They should not be dismissed as superstition or replaced by Western clinical models.

When family reunification is not possible (family deceased, rejected, or in a context too dangerous for return), alternative community arrangements should replicate family structures: foster care with community families, not group homes. Group homes for former CAAFAG create peer groups of other former armed group members, which can replicate armed group dynamics and increase re-recruitment risk. If group care is unavoidable, it must be time-limited with a clear plan for individual family placement.

Family tracing is a protection-sensitive activity: information about a child's location, identity, and status is sensitive and could endanger the child or family if it reaches former commanders or communities hostile to the child. Coordinate family tracing with ICRC (which has the most robust neutral family tracing infrastructure globally) and protect data according to strict data protection protocols. Do not share child case information across agencies without explicit consent and a clear legal basis.

The IASC Guidelines on Mental Health and Psychosocial Support in Emergency Settings provide the foundational framework. The intervention pyramid has four levels: (1) basic services and security for all; (2) community and family supports; (3) focused, non-specialized supports; (4) specialized services. Most former CAAFAG need levels 1-3, not level 4 clinical treatment. Over-medicalization — diagnosing and treating children as PTSD patients who need psychiatric medication — is a common error that can pathologize normal responses to abnormal experiences and undermine children's own resilience.

Social support is the strongest predictor of psychosocial recovery in former CAAFAG — stronger than the type or severity of abuses experienced. The Betancourt Sierra Leone longitudinal study found that acceptance by family and community, and access to economic opportunities, predicted adult adjustment far better than any clinical intervention or the content of what children experienced. This means: investing in community acceptance work, economic inclusion, and education access produces better mental health outcomes than investing in clinical counseling alone.

Culturally adapted psychosocial programming is not optional. Western-derived psychological concepts (PTSD, trauma processing, narrative exposure therapy) may not map onto local conceptual frameworks for distress. In many contexts, problems experienced after armed group membership are understood as spiritual, relational, or identity-based — not psychological in a clinical sense. Engage local healers, religious leaders, and community elders as co-designers of psychosocial programming, not as cultural 'translators' of Western models.

Structured activities with peers — sports, arts, group learning, agricultural work — produce robust psychosocial benefits for former CAAFAG and should not be dismissed as 'recreational.' They provide safe spaces to explore identity, build non-armed-group peer relationships, develop competence, and experience normalcy. Activity-based programming is especially important for children who are not ready or willing to engage in explicit 'counseling' — it allows relationship-building with caseworkers without the stigma and discomfort of direct trauma processing.

Substance use is a significant comorbidity in CAAFAG populations: many children are introduced to drugs and alcohol by armed groups as a way to reduce inhibition, build dependency, and increase compliance. Screening for substance use should be routine in intake assessment. Standard reintegration programming does not typically address substance use — this is a gap that frequently undermines other reintegration outcomes. Where possible, integrate substance use support into community-based programming rather than creating separate clinical facilities that children may refuse to attend.

⚠️ Secondary traumatic stress in practitioners: staff working with former CAAFAG — particularly children who disclose atrocities they perpetrated or experienced — are at high risk of vicarious trauma, secondary traumatic stress, and burnout. Mandatory supervision, regular case review, rotation of highest-burden cases, and organizational support for staff wellbeing are not luxuries — they are operational requirements. Organizations that do not invest in staff wellbeing consistently produce lower-quality casework and higher staff turnover, which is directly harmful to children who need consistent relationships.

Research on daily life in reintegration (Annan et al., 2009, Uganda) found that access to ordinary civilian routines — cooking family meals, farming, attending school, building friendships outside the armed group context — was among the strongest subjective indicators of reintegration progress reported by former child soldiers themselves. This reframes the psychosocial support task: the primary intervention is not clinical but structural — ensuring children have access to the fabric of ordinary civilian life. Programs that cannot place children in functioning households with economic stability and peer social life cannot compensate through counseling alone. Psychosocial programming and material/social structure programming are codependent, not alternatives.

lessons.category: Girls Associated with Armed Forces and Groups (GAAFG)
lessons: Girls are the most systematically underserved population in CAAFAG programming. Standard DDR program design — based on male combatant profiles, weapons handover verification, cantonment, and vocational training for traditionally male trades — excludes girls at every stage. Girls who were abducted as young children, used primarily for domestic labor and sexual services, and who never held a weapon are typically the girls with the greatest vulnerability and the least access to formal DDR eligibility. Design programs from the start with GAAFG as a primary beneficiary population, not an afterthought.

Sexual violence and forced marriage are near-universal experiences for GAAFG — not exceptional cases. Assessment and programming must treat these as the norm, not as special circumstances requiring referral. This means: routine, non-stigmatizing screening for sexual and reproductive health needs at all stages; access to family planning and maternal health services without requiring disclosure of the circumstances of pregnancy; psychosocial support specifically designed for survivors of sexual violence as a standard component, not a specialized add-on.

Girls face a specific double-rejection dynamic: rejected by communities for their association with armed groups (and for sexual abuse that communities may attribute to the girl's character rather than to perpetrator responsibility), and rejected by formal DDR systems for lack of a weapon to surrender. The Paris Principles explicitly address this but implementation is weak. Advocacy with DDR program designers, government DDR commissions, and donors to fund GAAFG-specific programming tracks is required — it will not happen without it.

Children born in captivity to GAAFG are a distinct and growing category of child at risk. These children have no status within the community of origin (may be seen as the 'children of the enemy'), no status within the armed group (after the mother's separation), and their mothers are adolescent girls who are themselves in need of intensive support. Both the child and the mother need individual case plans. The child's rights — to a name, nationality, family, education, and protection from discrimination — must be independently protected. Do not allow the child's status to be determined by the armed group's identity.

Vocational training for girls must go beyond 'female' trades (sewing, soap-making) and include livelihoods that produce viable income. Research consistently shows that skills training in low-income gendered trades does not produce economic independence and does not prevent re-recruitment. Identify the viable livelihood pathways available to girls in the specific return community and train toward those. Economic independence is a protective factor against re-recruitment and re-victimization — it is not a secondary reintegration objective.

The intersectional dimension of GAAFG programming is a critical gap in most programs. The Princeton/OSRSG-CAAC 2025 working paper identifies that girls with disabilities, girls from ethnic or religious minorities, girls who are LGBTQ+, and girls with very young children face compounded barriers that standard GAAFG programming does not address. A girl who is also visibly disabled, or who belongs to an ethnic group targeted by the community of return, faces rejection at multiple levels simultaneously — programming designed for 'girls' as a uniform category will not reach her. Conduct intersectional risk mapping for each GAAFG case: which dimensions of her identity and experience compound her vulnerability, and which community-level factors interact with each.

Quantitative research on gender differences in reintegration in Northern Uganda (Annan et al., 2011, Journal of Conflict Resolution) found that female CAAFAG had significantly worse economic outcomes than male CAAFAG even after controlling for nature and duration of armed group membership — and that standard DDR program participation did not close this gap. Girls who participated in formal DDR programs did not show significantly better economic outcomes than girls who did not. This challenges the assumption that DDR program access is the primary variable for girls' reintegration outcomes. It suggests the economic environment in return communities, family acceptance, and early marriage dynamics outweigh program effects for many girls. Address structural economic and social barriers in return communities alongside individual skill development.

Community engagement is not preparation for reintegration — it is reintegration. A child who has been individually healed, educated, and vocationally trained but returns to a community that rejects them will fail to reintegrate. A child with ongoing trauma and few skills who returns to a genuinely accepting community is more likely to reintegrate successfully. Allocate resources accordingly — community engagement programming should receive at least equivalent investment to individual child support.

Community sensitization must address both why communities may reject former CAAFAG (fear, anger, grief, stigma) and what communities gain from accepting returning children (family unity, community cohesion, reduced re-recruitment risk). Approaches that only explain the child's victimhood frequently do not work — they do not acknowledge the real harm that some children caused or the real fear that communities have. More effective approaches include: structured community dialogues, survivor-centered acknowledgment processes, visible community service by returning children, and economic benefits from reintegration programs (livelihood support that benefits the community, not only the child).

Teachers, religious leaders, and local government officials are the key community influencers — not just parents. A teacher who refuses to enroll a former child soldier sends a message that overrides any program sensitization. A religious leader who publicly welcomes returning children creates community permission for acceptance. Map the key influencers in each return community before reunification and invest in their preparation.

Peer mediation programs — where former CAAFAG and community youth engage together in structured dialogue and shared activity — produce stronger reintegration outcomes than top-down sensitization. The Betancourt Sierra Leone study found that peer acceptance among age-mates was among the strongest predictors of psychosocial adjustment. Design peer engagement programs that give community youth agency in the reintegration process, not just as passive recipients of sensitization messages.

Communities that themselves experienced violence at the hands of returning children require specific engagement. In LRA contexts in Uganda, some children returned to communities where they had killed or maimed neighbors. Rushing these children home without community preparation produced rejection, violence against returning children, and in some cases, re-departure to armed groups. Invest in community preparation proportional to the level of harm that occurred. Engage traditional accountability mechanisms where they exist and are appropriate — not to punish children, but to create community processes for acknowledgment and reintegration.

The African Union's post-conflict reconstruction and development guidelines identify community mobilization and local ownership as prerequisite conditions for effective DDR: community leaders and local authorities must actively support the return process, not merely tolerate it. Programs that bring in resources for returning children without engaging community structures for co-ownership frequently produce resentment — communities see returning CAAFAG as receiving benefits that other conflict-affected children and families do not receive. Design reintegration programs with visible community-wide benefits — infrastructure, community health support, school supplies — that create concrete incentives for community stakeholders to invest in the success of returning children rather than viewing them as competitors for scarce resources.

lessons.category: Education, Livelihoods, and Prevention of Re-recruitment
lessons: Education access is simultaneously a reintegration outcome and the most powerful prevention against re-recruitment. Former CAAFAG who are in school are significantly less likely to be re-recruited than those who are not (multiple studies, including Betancourt Sierra Leone). Yet former CAAFAG often face multiple barriers to education: age-grade mismatches (a 15-year-old who missed four years of school cannot enter a classroom with 11-year-olds without stigma and shame), school fees, lack of documentation, and teacher and peer rejection. Flexible, accelerated learning programs that allow age-appropriate entry into the school system are more effective than pushing children into grade-level classes where they will fail and drop out.

Livelihoods programming for adolescent CAAFAG must produce economic independence, not skill certificates. The most common failure in vocational training programs is providing training in skills for which there is no local market demand, or providing training without the tools, materials, or capital to practice the skill after the program ends. Before designing vocational training, conduct a local market assessment. Provide starter kits and business skills alongside technical training. Follow up at 6 and 12 months to assess income generation — not just skill acquisition.

Re-recruitment is the most concrete measure of reintegration failure — and it is much more common than program data typically reflects. The known risk factors for re-recruitment: (1) community rejection or family rejection; (2) lack of economic alternatives; (3) ongoing contact with former commanders; (4) presence of active armed group recruitment in the return community; (5) unaddressed substance use; (6) returning to a community where they committed violence without reconciliation processes. Case plans should explicitly map each child against these risk factors and include mitigation actions for each.

Former commanders retain leverage over children long after formal separation. This is not only through fear — it is through identity, belonging, and economic dependency. Children who experienced genuine community within armed groups (belonging, purpose, status) and who return to communities where they are rejected and economically marginal may be susceptible to re-recruitment not because they are coerced but because the armed group offers something civilian life does not. Reintegration programming must provide genuine alternative structures of belonging, purpose, and status — not just material support.

Re-recruitment prevention requires engaging the armed group context, not only the individual child. If the armed group is still active in or near the return community, children face recruitment pressure regardless of their individual resilience or program participation. Advocate for access monitoring and protection mechanisms in communities with active armed group presence near return areas. Coordinate with child protection cluster partners and peacekeeping forces (where present) on early warning mechanisms for re-recruitment.

ILO-IPEC research on economic reintegration (2010), drawing on DRC and Philippines case studies, identifies four vocational training modalities with substantially different outcome profiles: (1) center-based skills training — most commonly funded, lowest income generation outcomes due to disconnect from local labor markets; (2) apprenticeships with local artisans and small businesses — produces higher employment rates because children build relationships with employers during training; (3) agricultural livelihoods packages — most effective for rural return contexts, but must include land access and starter inputs, not training alone; (4) microenterprise support with business skills and seed capital — highest income potential but requires children with basic literacy and numeracy and consistent follow-up mentoring. The programming error is choosing a modality based on funding availability or organizational capacity rather than on the child's return context. Match modality to where the child is going and what the local economy can absorb.

lessons.category: Justice, Accountability, and the Child's Dual Status
lessons: Former CAAFAG have a dual status in international and domestic law: they are both victims (of recruitment and use, frequently of violence and sexual abuse) and, in some cases, perpetrators (of acts that would be crimes if committed by adults). How to hold this tension is the most legally and ethically complex dimension of CAAFAG programming. The international consensus, reflected in the Paris Principles, UNCRC, and SCR 2427, is that children should be treated primarily as victims, not perpetrators. This does not mean accountability is impossible — it means it must be pursued through child-appropriate, rehabilitative frameworks.

Prosecuting children for acts committed as armed group members is legally and ethically problematic under international law. The Optional Protocol to the CRC on children in armed conflict obliges states to take all feasible measures to prevent the recruitment and use of children in armed conflict — it does not create criminal liability for children. When children are prosecuted (as in the DRC, Sierra Leone's special court, and Uganda's ICC referrals), outcomes are mixed. The Child Soldiers International and Human Rights Watch positions are that criminal prosecution of children for acts committed as child soldiers should be the last resort, pursued only for the most serious cases, and only through juvenile justice systems with full procedural safeguards.

Truth and reconciliation mechanisms can be valuable for former CAAFAG — but their design matters enormously. The Sierra Leone TRC included special hearings for children with specific protections (not naming children in public records, age-appropriate facilitation). When TRC processes allow children to speak their truth without prosecution, acknowledge their victimhood and their actions, and receive community response, they can support reintegration. When TRC processes treat children as perpetrators and expose them to community hostility, they can destroy reintegration progress. Advocate for child-specific safeguards in any transitional justice mechanism.

Traditional justice and reconciliation mechanisms should be considered for children — not as an alternative to child protection standards but as a complement. In many contexts, traditional mechanisms (cleansing ceremonies, community apology processes, elder-mediated negotiation) are more accessible, more culturally legitimate, and produce more durable community acceptance than formal justice processes. They should not be used to impose punishment, to waive children's rights, or to expose them to community violence — but well-designed engagement of traditional mechanisms can achieve community reconciliation that formal justice cannot.

Accountability for recruitment — not for the child's actions but for the recruiters — is a protection priority. Adult commanders who recruited, used, or sexually exploited children are criminally liable under international law. Supporting ICC referrals, universal jurisdiction prosecutions, and national prosecution of recruiters is an appropriate activity for protection organizations and does not conflict with support for child reintegration. Accountability of recruiters also has a deterrence effect on future recruitment.

The UN SG's 2024 report on the Central African Republic (S/2024/93) documents a pattern that is increasingly common in fragmented conflicts: over 14,000 children were verified as released from armed groups between 2014-2023 in CAR, yet recruitment by the same groups continued simultaneously — and the same children sometimes cycled through multiple groups. This pattern — where release programs and continued recruitment operate in parallel — challenges the logic of DDR as a linear, one-time process. In high-fluidity conflict environments, reintegration programming must be designed for potential cycling: children who return to armed groups after release should be re-admissible to programming without stigmatization or punitive barriers. One-strike eligibility policies undermine both humanitarian principles and practical effectiveness when the armed group is still active and community conditions have not changed.

Do no harm is not a passive principle in CAAFAG work — it is an active assessment discipline. The most common 'harms' inflicted by well-intentioned programs: (1) labeling children as 'child soldiers' in ways that follow them and create stigma; (2) creating group identities of former CAAFAG that replicate armed group peer dynamics; (3) rushing reunification without preparation; (4) conducting poorly facilitated group trauma processing sessions that re-traumatize; (5) promising individual support that programs cannot sustain. Before launching any activity, assess the specific harm pathway it could create and build in mitigations.

Confidentiality and data protection are life-or-death issues in CAAFAG work. Children's names, locations, case histories, and CAAFAG status must be protected with the same rigor as any other sensitive personal data — more so, because disclosure to former commanders, hostile community members, or media can endanger children or their families. Establish data protection protocols before beginning work: who can access case files, how files are stored (not in Excel spreadsheets on laptops that travel to the field), what data can be shared across agencies, and what the deletion schedule is.

Media access to former CAAFAG requires specific protocols. Journalists and documentary filmmakers frequently seek access to 'child soldier stories' that can produce harmful coverage — identifying children, exposing their histories, creating images that follow children into adult life. The Paris Principles and child rights frameworks prohibit identifying children in media without free, prior, and informed consent by the child and parent/guardian (where available), and after assessment that disclosure will not harm the child. Establish a media policy at the organizational level and brief field staff on how to handle unsolicited media requests.

⚠️ Staff security in contexts with active armed group presence: organizations that operate reintegration programs in areas where armed groups are still active face specific risks. Armed groups have raided interim care facilities, re-recruited children from program sites, and targeted staff of organizations that negotiate child releases. Risk assessment must account for the organization's profile with specific armed groups. Organizations that have successfully negotiated releases may be seen as legitimate humanitarians — or as adversaries. Know how each armed group perceives your organization before establishing fixed program sites.

Long-term follow-up is the most resource-constrained and most critical component of reintegration programming. The Betancourt longitudinal study tracked children across 16 years and found that outcomes continued to change — children who appeared to have failed reintegration at 18 months had improved substantially by age 25 in some cases. Program funding cycles (typically 1-3 years) are incompatible with the reintegration timeline (5-10+ years for full social and economic integration). Advocate for longer funding cycles. When funding ends, transition children to community-based support systems — schools, community youth groups, religious organizations — that can sustain relationships after the program closes.

The Princeton/OSRSG-CAAC 2025 working paper establishes organizational accountability requirements for gender-responsive reintegration programming that go beyond individual practitioner competency: (1) organizations must collect sex- and gender-disaggregated data at every program stage and review it against programmatic benchmarks — not just at baseline; (2) program designs must pass a gender equality and intersectionality review before implementation begins; (3) organizations must designate a specific accountability focal point for GAAFG programming — a named coordinator with authority, not merely a 'gender mainstreaming' responsibility distributed across staff roles; (4) training on gender-responsive programming must include all staff who interact with CAAFAG — not only female caseworkers — since male staff regularly conduct assessment, transit, and community follow-up with GAAFG. These requirements mark a shift from gender sensitivity as an attitude to gender responsiveness as a measurable institutional practice that can be audited and improved.
